segments.inferno-9-g9: This target contains only punctuation and no lexical or grammatical material to explain.
segments.inferno-9-g10: Tal is a substantivized poetic adjective, meaning “such a one,” referring elliptically to a powerful helper already implied by the broken speech. Ne is an archaic clitic here used with the value “to us,” while s’offerse is si offerse, the 3rd-person singular passato remoto of offerirsi, “offered herself/himself.”
segments.inferno-9-g11: Quanto is an exclamative degree word with tarda, whose idiomatic sense here is “seems long” rather than simply “delays”; a me marks the experiencer, “to me.” Ch’altri qui giunga introduces the subject/content clause, with altri meaning “another person” and giunga in the present subjunctive after the dependent che-clause.
segments.inferno-9-g12: This target contains only punctuation and no lexical or grammatical material to explain.
segments.inferno-9-g1: “Quel color” is the subject of the whole sentence, expanded by the relative clause “che viltà ... mi pinse,” where “viltà” is the subject and “che” is the object: the color that cowardice painted on Dante’s exterior. “Veggendo” is a gerund meaning “on seeing,” with Dante as its implied subject, and “tornare in volta” means “turn back.”
segments.inferno-9-g2: This is the main predicate: “ristrinse” has “Quel color” as its subject and “il suo novo” as its object, with “color” understood after “novo.” “Più tosto” means “more quickly,” and “dentro” means that Virgil forced or kept his own new color inward, concealing his fear or disturbance.
segments.inferno-9-g3: “Attento” is a predicative adjective describing the subject as intent or attentive. “Si fermò” is a pronominal/reflexive form meaning “he stopped,” and “com’ uom ch’ascolta” is a comparison: “like a man who listens.”
segments.inferno-9-g4: “Ché” introduces the reason for his stopping: his eye or sight could not carry him far. “Nol” is the contraction of “non lo,” and “a lunga” is an adverbial expression meaning “far”; the two “per” phrases describe the obstructing medium, the black air and thick fog.
segments.inferno-9-g5: “Pur” has concessive force here, roughly “nevertheless” or “all the same.” “A noi converrà” is an impersonal future construction meaning “it will be necessary for us,” followed by the infinitive “vincer”; “la punga” is an archaic form meaning the struggle or contest to be overcome.
segments.inferno-9-g6: “Cominciò” introduces the quoted speech and means “he began.” “El” is an archaic form for “he,” referring to Virgil.
segments.inferno-9-g7: “Se non” begins an unfinished conditional phrase, literally “if not” or “unless,” and the interruption is marked by the broken-off punctuation. The listed sense for “se” does not match this use, since here it is the conjunction “if.”
segments.inferno-9-g8: This segment is a single sentence-final period (full stop) and therefore pure punctuation, not a lexical item. It indicates an abrupt stop or the end of the quoted/interrupted speech in the text; it has no grammatical features like tense, mood, or word order to analyze.
segments.inferno-9-g84: “Ben” strengthens the statement, meaning “clearly” or “indeed.” “M’accorsi” is the passato remoto of reflexive “accorgersi,” “I realized,” followed by “ch’” introducing the object clause; “elli” is an archaic singular “he,” and “era ... messo” is a passive construction, literally “was sent/placed,” with “da ciel” expressing heavenly source or agency.
segments.inferno-9-g85: “Volsimi” is an enclitic form: “volsi” + “mi,” literally “I turned myself,” with the pronoun attached after the finite verb in poetic style. “Al maestro” means “to the master,” referring to Dante’s guide, Virgil.
segments.inferno-9-g86: “Quei” means “that one,” here Virgil, and “fé segno” is the shortened poetic form of “fece segno,” “made a sign.” The “ch’” clause gives the content/purpose of the sign: “i’” is elided “io,” while “stessi” and “inchinassi” are imperfect subjunctives after the command-like gesture, meaning “that I should stay quiet and bow to him.”
segments.inferno-9-g87: “Ahi” is an emotional exclamation, and “quanto” introduces an exclamatory degree phrase, “how.” “Mi parea” means “he seemed to me,” with “parea” as the poetic imperfect form of “pareva”; “pien di disdegno” is a compact adjective phrase, “full of disdain.”
segments.inferno-9-g88: “Venne” is passato remoto, narrating the angel’s completed action: “he came.” “A la porta” is the older separated form of modern “alla porta,” and “l’aperse” contains an elided object pronoun referring back to “porta,” so the sense is “he opened it”; “con una verghetta” gives the instrument, “with/by means of a little rod.”
segments.inferno-9-g89: Here “che” introduces an explanatory or result-like clause, close to “for” or “since.” “V’ebbe” is “vi ebbe,” literally “there had/was,” an existential use of “avere”; with “non ... alcun,” the phrase means “there was no check/resistance at all.”
segments.inferno-9-g90: “O” marks direct address, and “cacciati” is a masculine plural past participle used substantively: “you who were driven out.” “Del ciel” contracts “di + il” and here means “from heaven,” identifying the place from which they were expelled.
segments.inferno-9-g91: “Gente” is a collective noun used in direct address, “people” or “race.” “Dispetta” is an adjective agreeing with “gente,” meaning something like “despised,” “hateful,” or “accursed,” though the supplied noun senses do not match this adjectival use exactly.
segments.inferno-9-g92: “Cominciò elli” means “he began,” with “elli” as an archaic subject pronoun for “he.” “In su l’orribil soglia” is a poetic prepositional phrase, literally “on/upon the horrible threshold,” with “orribil” an apocopated form before the noun.
segments.inferno-9-g93: “Ond’” means “whence” or “from where,” introducing a rhetorical question. “Esta” is an archaic form of “questa,” and “s’alletta” is reflexive “si alletta,” literally “beds/lodges itself,” so the question asks where this arrogance finds a place within them.
segments.inferno-9-g13: “I’” is an elided form of “io,” though the word list misidentifies it; the subject is Dante, with “vidi” as a passato remoto: “I saw/perceived.” “Sì com’” means “just how,” and “lo cominciar” is a nominalized infinitive, “the beginning”; “l’altro che poi venne” means the later part of Virgil’s speech, literally “the other that afterwards came.”
segments.inferno-9-g14: The relative “che” refers back to the later words just mentioned: they “were words different from the first.” “Fur” is the syncopated passato remoto form of “furono,” and “a le prime” is archaic spelling for modern “alle prime,” here meaning “from/in relation to the first ones.”
segments.inferno-9-g15: “Ma nondimen” introduces a concession: despite Virgil’s correcting words, fear remained. “Il suo dir” is a nominalized infinitive, “his saying/speech,” and “dienne” is an archaic compound form meaning “gave us,” with enclitic “ne” understood as “to us.”
segments.inferno-9-g16: “Perch’” means “because,” giving the reason for the fear. “Traeva” is imperfect, “I was drawing/deriving,” and “la parola tronca” is the interrupted or cut-off utterance; “che non tenne” is a comparative construction, with “non” pleonastic after “peggior,” meaning “than it actually contained/held.”
segments.inferno-9-g17: The prepositional phrase “In questo fondo de la trista conca” is fronted before the verb for emphasis: “into this depth of the sad/wretched hollow.” “Discende mai alcun” is interrogative word order, with “mai” meaning “ever” and “alcun” meaning “anyone”; “del primo grado” means “from the first rank/circle,” referring to Limbo.
segments.inferno-9-g18: The relative “che” refers to “alcun del primo grado,” someone from the first circle. “Sol” is an apocopated form of “solo,” “only,” not the solar adjective suggested by the lemma; “ha la speranza cionca” literally says he “has hope maimed,” meaning his punishment is deprivation of hope.
segments.inferno-9-g19: This segment contains only punctuation and no grammatical content to explain.
segments.inferno-9-g20: “Questa question” is an archaic or poetic form for “questa questione,” the object placed before the verb. “Fec’” is elided “feci,” passato remoto of “fare,” used idiomatically as “I made/asked” a question, followed by the explicit subject “io” after the verb.
segments.inferno-9-g21: “Quei” means “that one/he,” referring to Virgil, and introduces his direct speech. “Di rado” is an idiom meaning “rarely,” while “incontra” is impersonal: “it happens.”
segments.inferno-9-g22: “Mi” is an indirect object, “to me.” “Rispuose” is an archaic form of “rispose,” passato remoto of “rispondere,” placed after the quoted words in an inverted reporting clause: “he replied to me.”
segments.inferno-9-g23: “Che” introduces the content clause dependent on impersonal “incontra”: “that it happens that...” “Faccia” is present subjunctive after this construction, and “fare il cammino” means “make/take the journey”; “per qual io vado” is elliptical for “per lo quale io vado,” “by/through which I go.”
segments.inferno-9-g55: “Vegna” is an archaic present subjunctive used optatively: “may/let ... come.” The subject follows the verb, “Medusa,” giving the cry a dramatic, imperative force.
segments.inferno-9-g56: “Sì” means “so/then” here, linking Medusa’s arrival to the result. “’l” is a contracted object pronoun for “lo,” not the article in sense, and “farem” is a shortened future form of “faremo”; “di smalto” means turning him into a hard, enamel-like substance, i.e. petrifying him.
segments.inferno-9-g57: “Dicevan” is the imperfect, describing what they were saying repeatedly or continuously. “Tutte” is the plural subject, and the gerund “riguardando” gives the simultaneous action: they say this while looking “in giuso,” down below.
segments.inferno-9-g58: “Mal” is adverbial, meaning “badly/ill,” so the phrase means “we did wrong.” “Vengiammo” is an archaic past form meaning “we avenged,” with “non” giving “we did not avenge”; “in Tesëo” means upon/in the person of Theseus, and “l’assalto” is the assault or attack connected with him.
segments.inferno-9-g59: “Volgiti” is an imperative with the reflexive pronoun attached: “turn yourself,” i.e. “turn around.” “’n dietro” is a contraction for “in dietro,” and “tien” is the shortened imperative of “tenere,” governing “lo viso chiuso”: keep the face/eyes shut or covered.
segments.inferno-9-g60: “Ché” introduces the reason for the command: “for/because.” “Se” is the conditional conjunction “if,” though that sense is not in the supplied list; “si mostra” is reflexive/pronominal, “shows herself/appears,” and “vedessi” is imperfect subjunctive, expressing a feared hypothetical possibility.
segments.inferno-9-g61: “Nulla sarebbe” literally means “there would be nothing,” used idiomatically for “there would be no possibility.” “Di tornar mai suso” depends on “nulla”: no chance of ever returning upward; “tornar” is an infinitive and “suso” is the archaic form for “up/above.”
segments.inferno-9-g62: “Così” points back to the warning just spoken: “thus/in this way.” “Disse” is the simple past, and “’l maestro” is the contracted form of “il maestro,” referring to Virgil as Dante’s guide and teacher.
segments.inferno-9-g63: “Elli stessi” is archaic for “he himself,” with an emphatic pronoun after the subject. “Mi volse” means “turned me,” but “volse” here is from “volgere,” not from the listed senses of “volere/vogliere.”
segments.inferno-9-g64: “Non si tenne a le mie mani” literally means “he did not hold himself to my hands,” an idiom meaning he did not rely on, or was not satisfied with, Dante’s own hands covering his face. “A le” is the uncontracted form corresponding to later “alle.”
segments.inferno-9-g65: Here “che ... non” has the force of “without also”: he did not merely leave it to Dante’s hands, but also closed/covered him with his own. “Chiudessi” is an archaic third-person imperfect subjunctive form, used where modern Italian would expect “chiudesse”; the object “mi” means Dante himself, especially his eyes/face.
segments.inferno-9-g66: This is a direct apostrophe to the readers: “O voi,” “O you.” “Ch’avete” contracts “che avete,” a relative clause meaning “who have,” and “li ’ntelletti sani” uses the old article “li” with a syncopated form of “intelletti”: sound, healthy minds.
segments.inferno-9-g67: “Mirate” is an imperative plural, urging the readers to look carefully or contemplate. “Che s’asconde” is a relative clause, “which hides itself/is hidden,” and “sotto ’l velame de li versi strani” means beneath the veil or covering formed by these strange verses.
segments.inferno-9-g24: “Ver è che” is an archaic/poetic way of saying “it is true that,” with the adjective “Ver” placed first for emphasis. “Altra fïata” means “another time/occasion,” and “fui” is the first-person singular passato remoto of “essere,” here meaning “I was”; “qua giù” means “down here.”
segments.inferno-9-g25: “Congiurato” is a past participle agreeing with the implied “io” and is passive in force: “conjured/summoned.” “Da quella Eritón cruda” gives the agent, “by that cruel Erichtho,” and the relative clause “che richiamava...” uses the imperfect “richiamava” for her habitual necromantic action of calling shades back to their bodies; “sui” is an archaic possessive form meaning “their own.”
segments.inferno-9-g26: “Di poco” is an idiom meaning “for only a little while” or “only recently.” The syntax is inverted and poetic: literally “the flesh was bare of me,” meaning the speaker’s body had only recently been deprived of his soul, that is, he had recently died.
segments.inferno-9-g27: Here “ch’” links the first clause to what happened next, with a temporal/consecutive sense close to “when.” “Ella mi fece intrar” is a causative construction: “she made me enter,” with “intrar” as the apocopated infinitive “entrare”; “dentr’ a quel muro” means “inside/within that wall.”
segments.inferno-9-g28: “Per” plus infinitive expresses purpose: “in order to.” “Trarne” combines “trarre” with the pronoun “ne,” meaning “to draw out from there/from it”; the object is “un spirto,” further specified by “del cerchio di Giuda,” “from the circle of Judas.”
segments.inferno-9-g29: “Quell’ è” means “that is,” with elision before the vowel. The repeated “’l più” forms superlatives: “the lowest place” and “the darkest,” with “loco” as an archaic form of “luogo,” “place.”
segments.inferno-9-g30: This continues the series of superlatives: “and the farthest from...” “Dal ciel” means “from the heaven/celestial sphere,” and the relative clause “che tutto gira” describes that heaven as the one that turns all things or sets all in motion.
segments.inferno-9-g31: “Ben” intensifies the verb: “well” or “thoroughly.” “So” is the first-person singular present of “sapere,” and “’l cammin” is the elided article plus “cammin,” a poetic form meaning “the path/way.”
segments.inferno-9-g32: “Però” here has its older causal-result sense, “therefore.” “Ti fa sicuro” uses the imperative “fa” from “fare” with reflexive “ti”: literally “make yourself secure,” meaning “be confident/reassured.”
segments.inferno-9-g33: “Questa palude” is the subject of the main verb “cigne,” an archaic form of “cinge,” meaning “encircles/girds.” The relative clause “che ’l gran puzzo spira” means “which breathes out the great stench,” and “dintorno” reinforces the idea of surrounding the “città dolente,” the sorrowful city.
segments.inferno-9-g34: “U’” is a poetic shortened form meaning “where,” referring back to the city. “Potemo” is an archaic form of “possiamo,” “we can,” and “omai” means “now/by now”; “sanz’ ira” is an elided form of “senza ira,” “without anger/wrath.”
segments.inferno-9-g130: This target contains only punctuation, so there is no grammar or vocabulary to analyze in the segment itself.
segments.inferno-9-g131: “E quelli a me” is an elliptical reporting formula: the verb of speaking is omitted but understood, so it means “And he [said] to me.” “Quelli” refers back to the guide, and “a me” marks the indirect object.
segments.inferno-9-g132: “Qui son” uses the shortened poetic form “son” for “sono,” meaning “are.” “Li” is an archaic masculine plural article, and “eresïarche” is the plural subject; “con lor seguaci” adds the accompanying group, with “lor” meaning “their.”
segments.inferno-9-g133: “D’ogne setta” is a prepositional phrase modifying the heresiarchs and their followers: they come “from/of every sect.” “Ogne” is an archaic form of modern Italian “ogni.”
segments.inferno-9-g134: The clause “e molto più che non credi” forms a comparison: literally “and much more than you do not think,” where “non” is pleonastic after the comparative and is not translated as a real negation. The main clause is “son le tombe carche,” with poetic word order: “the tombs are full/loaded.”
segments.inferno-9-g135: “Simile ... con simile” is a substantivized adjective construction, literally “like with like,” meaning similar sinners are grouped together. “È sepolto” is a passive construction with “essere” plus past participle: “is buried.”
segments.inferno-9-g136: “I monimenti” is an archaic or poetic form for “i monumenti,” here meaning the tombs or sepulchral structures. “Son più e men caldi” means they are hot to different degrees, literally “more and less hot.”
segments.inferno-9-g137: “Poi ch’” is the contracted form of “poi che,” used here as a temporal conjunction meaning “after.” “Si fu vòlto” is a reflexive compound past form, literally “he had turned himself,” with “fu” as auxiliary and “vòlto” as the past participle; “a la man destra” means “to the right-hand side.”
segments.inferno-9-g138: “Passammo” is first-person plural passato remoto, “we passed,” narrating the next completed action. “Tra i martìri e li alti spaldi” gives the path they took, between the punishments on one side and the high ramparts on the other; “li” is an archaic article for modern “gli/i.”
segments.inferno-9-g35: The sentence opens with a simple coordinated clause: “E” links this remark to what has just been said, and “disse” is the preterite of “dire,” giving a completed action. “Altro” is used substantively, meaning “something else” rather than modifying an expressed noun.
segments.inferno-9-g36: “Ma” introduces a contrast: although more was said, the narrator cannot recall it. “L’” is an elided object pronoun referring to what was said, not the article “the”; “ho a mente” is an idiom meaning “have in mind” or “remember.”
segments.inferno-9-g37: “Però che” is an archaic conjunction meaning “because,” explaining why he does not remember the words. “M’avea ... tratto” is a pluperfect construction, “had drawn me,” with “tutto” adverbially meaning “completely”; “ver’” is the poetic truncation of “verso,” “toward.”
segments.inferno-9-g38: “Dove” refers back to the tower-top and introduces a relative clause of place. “In un punto” means “in one moment, instantly”; “furon dritte” uses “furon” plus the adjective “dritte” to say that the Furies were suddenly upright or stood erect, while “ratto” functions adverbially, “quickly.”
segments.inferno-9-g39: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to the three Furies. “Avieno” is an archaic imperfect form of “avevano,” “they had,” and it governs both “membra feminine” and “atto,” meaning that their limbs and bearing were female.
segments.inferno-9-g40: This clause continues the description with another imperfect verb, “eran,” for “erano.” “Cinte” is a past participle used adjectivally/passively, and “con idre verdissime” gives what they were girded or wreathed with.
segments.inferno-9-g41: The subject is still the Furies, carried over from the preceding clauses. “Avien” is another archaic shortened imperfect, “avevano,” and “per crine” means “as hair” or “in place of hair,” with “per” marking function rather than purpose.
segments.inferno-9-g42: “Onde” here is not the noun “waves” but a relative adverb meaning “with which” or “whereby,” referring to the serpents just mentioned. “Erano avvinte” is an imperfect passive construction: their fierce temples were bound or entwined by those serpents.
segments.inferno-9-g43: “E” continues the narrative, and “quei” is a poetic demonstrative pronoun meaning “he,” referring to the guide. The main verb of this subject is delayed until “mi disse,” after the intervening relative clause.
segments.inferno-9-g44: This relative clause describes “quei”: he “well recognized” the figures. “Conobbe” is preterite, and “le meschine de la regina de l’etterno pianto” identifies them as the wretched attendants or servants of the queen of eternal weeping, with repeated “de” for modern “di.”
segments.inferno-9-g45: “Guarda” is a second-person singular imperative. It introduces direct speech and takes its object in the following words, interrupted by the reporting phrase “mi disse.”
segments.inferno-9-g46: “Mi disse” is the reporting clause inserted into the direct speech. “Mi” is an indirect object, “to me,” and “disse” is the preterite of “dire.”
segments.inferno-9-g47: This noun phrase completes the object of “Guarda”: “le feroci Erine,” the fierce Erinyes or Furies. “Erine” is the Italian form of the mythological Greek avenging spirits.
segments.inferno-9-g48: “Quest’” is an elided form of “questa,” used deictically: the speaker points out one of the three figures. “Dal sinistro canto” literally means “at/from the left corner,” with “canto” meaning side or corner rather than song.
segments.inferno-9-g49: “Quella che piange” is a relative construction: “the one who weeps.” “Dal destro” parallels “dal sinistro canto,” meaning “on the right side,” and the copula “è” identifies her as Alecto.
segments.inferno-9-g50: This final identification places Tisiphone “nel mezzo,” in the middle. “È” here has a locative force, not merely an identifying one, because it is followed by a place phrase.
segments.inferno-9-g51: After the direct speech closes, the narration resumes with “e tacque.” “A tanto” is an idiomatic phrase meaning “at that point” or “having said so much,” marking the end of the guide’s explanation.
segments.inferno-9-g52: “Con l’unghie” gives the instrument, “with the nails.” “Si fendea” is an archaic imperfect reflexive form, “was tearing herself,” and “ciascuna” is the singular subject, “each one,” so the singular verb agrees with it.
segments.inferno-9-g53: “Battiensi” is an enclitic/contracted form for “si batteano,” “they beat themselves.” “A palme” means “with their palms,” and “sì alto” means “so loudly,” with “alto” used adverbially for the height or volume of the cries.
segments.inferno-9-g54: “Ch’i’” contracts “che io,” introducing the result clause after “sì alto”: they cried so loudly that the narrator reacted. “Mi strinsi” is a reflexive preterite, “I pressed myself close,” and “per sospetto” means “because of fear” rather than mere intellectual suspicion.
segments.inferno-9-g68: The subject is placed after the verb: “un fracasso d’un suon” is what “venìa” was doing. “Venìa” is an archaic/poetic imperfect form of “veniva,” and “su per” means moving up over or along the waves.
segments.inferno-9-g69: “Pien” is a shortened poetic form of “pieno,” agreeing with “fracasso/suon” and followed by “di.” The phrase qualifies the terrible sound as being filled with terror.
segments.inferno-9-g70: “Per cui” means “because of/by which,” referring back to the terrifying sound. “Tremavano” is imperfect, describing the continuing effect of the noise on “amendue le sponde,” both banks of the Styx.
segments.inferno-9-g71: “Non altrimenti ... che” is a comparative construction meaning “in no other way than,” or “just like.” “Fatto” refers back to the sound as being produced like the noise of a violent wind; “avversi ardori” means opposing heats, though the supplied lemma for “avversi” does not match this adjective sense.
segments.inferno-9-g72: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to the wind. “Fier” is a poetic verb form meaning “strikes,” not the adjective “fierce”; “sanz’” is an archaic contraction of “senza,” and “schianta” is a present-tense verb meaning “splinters” or “breaks apart.”
segments.inferno-9-g73: The verbs “abbatte” and “porta” continue the same relative clause about the wind. “Fori” is an archaic form of “fuori,” here meaning that the wind carries the branches out or away.
segments.inferno-9-g74: The subject is still the wind, described in a personifying way as going forward “superbo,” proudly. “Polveroso” functions adjectivally, suggesting the wind is dusty or raises dust as it advances.
segments.inferno-9-g75: “Fa fuggir” is a causative construction: literally “makes flee.” “Fiere” here means wild beasts, but that noun sense is not among the supplied options, so it is marked -1.
segments.inferno-9-g76: “Li occhi” uses the old masculine plural article “li” for modern “gli.” “Mi sciolse” literally means “he released/unbound to me,” with “mi” functioning like a possessive dative: he freed my eyes.
segments.inferno-9-g77: “Drizza” is an imperative: Virgil commands Dante to direct his gaze. “Il nerbo del viso” is a metaphor for the force or sinew of sight; “indi” here means “there/thither,” and “fummo” is an archaic form of “fumo,” smoke, neither of which matches the supplied sense lists.
segments.inferno-9-g78: “Come” begins a simile whose main clause comes later with “vid’ io.” “Innanzi a la nimica biscia” means before or in the presence of the hostile snake, and “si dileguan” is a pronominal/reflexive-style verb meaning that the frogs vanish or scatter away.
segments.inferno-9-g79: “Fin ch’” is the contraction of “fin che,” meaning “until.” “Ciascuna s’abbica” has singular agreement after the earlier plural “rane”: each frog individually huddles or heaps itself on the land.
segments.inferno-9-g80: “Vid’ io” is a poetic elision and inversion for “vidi io,” “I saw.” The infinitive “fuggir” depends on “vid’ io” in the construction “I saw them flee”; “ch’al passo passava Stige” is a relative clause describing the one who was crossing the Styx at the ford, with dry soles.
segments.inferno-9-g81: The subject is the heavenly messenger described in the preceding sentence. “Rimovea” is an archaic imperfect form of “rimuoveva,” and “quell’ aere grasso” means that thick, heavy air around his face.
segments.inferno-9-g82: “Menando” is a gerund explaining how he cleared the air: by moving or bringing his left hand forward. “La sinistra” is elliptical for “the left hand,” and “spesso” functions adverbially, “often/repeatedly.”
segments.inferno-9-g83: “Parea” is an archaic imperfect form of “pareva,” “seemed.” “Sol di quell’ angoscia” means he seemed weary only from that oppressive nuisance, not from fear or exertion.
segments.inferno-9-g94: “Perché” introduces a direct question: “why?” “Recalcitrate a” takes the preposition “a,” meaning “resist/kick against” something; “quella voglia” is the divine will. The relative phrase “a cui” links back to “voglia,” and “non puote ... mai esser mozzo” uses “puote” as an archaic form of “può,” with “esser mozzo” as a passive infinitive: its end can never be cut off.
segments.inferno-9-g95: “E che” continues the relative clause, again referring to “quella voglia”: “and which.” “Più volte” means “many times,” and “v’ha” is “vi ha,” with “vi” meaning “you/to you”; “ha cresciuta doglia” is a compound past form meaning that this will has increased pain for them.
segments.inferno-9-g96: “Che giova” means “what does it help/what good is it,” with “che” as interrogative “what.” “Ne le” is the older separated form of modern “nelle,” “in/against the,” and “le fata” is an archaic plural for “the fates.” “Dar di cozzo” is an idiom meaning to collide with, butt against, or violently oppose something.
segments.inferno-9-g97: “Cerbero vostro” is the subject of the sentence, placed first for emphasis: “your Cerberus.” “Vostro” addresses the infernal powers as if Cerberus belongs to their side.
segments.inferno-9-g98: “Se ben vi ricorda” is an archaic impersonal construction meaning “if you remember well.” The pronoun “vi” refers to the addressees, and “ricorda” is singular because the construction is literally something like “if it remembers well to you.”
segments.inferno-9-g99: “Ne” points back to the consequence or evidence of that earlier resistance, roughly “from it/as a result of it.” “Porta ... pelato il mento e ’l gozzo” means that Cerberus still bears or wears his chin and throat stripped bare; “’l” is the contracted form of “il.”
segments.inferno-9-g100: “Poi” marks the next action in sequence. “Si rivolse” is a reflexive/simple past form meaning “he turned himself/turned back,” and “per la strada lorda” gives the route, “along the filthy road.”
segments.inferno-9-g101: “Non fé motto a noi” means “he did not say a word to us.” “Fé” is an apocopated poetic form of “fece,” the passato remoto of “fare,” and “far motto” is an idiom for speaking or uttering a word.
segments.inferno-9-g102: “Ma fé sembiante d’omo” means “but he made/showed the appearance of a man,” that is, he seemed like such a man. “Cui altra cura stringa e morda” uses subjunctive verbs to characterize a person hypothetically: one whom another anxiety grips and bites. “Che quella di colui che li è davante” is comparative, “than that concerning the one who is before him,” with “che” meaning “than.”
segments.inferno-9-g103: “E noi” shifts the action back to Dante and Virgil. “Movemmo” is passato remoto, first-person plural, “we moved,” and “movemmo i piedi” is a concrete idiom for setting out or walking. “Inver’ la terra” means “toward the city/fortified place,” with “terra” used in the medieval sense of a town or city.
segments.inferno-9-g104: “Sicuri” is a plural adjective agreeing with “noi,” meaning reassured or confident. “Appresso le parole sante” means “after” or “following upon the holy words,” referring grammatically to the words just spoken as the cause of their confidence.
segments.inferno-9-g105: “Dentro li ’ntrammo” uses poetic word order: the adverb “Dentro” is fronted, and “li” is the old masculine plural article referring back to the gates/walls just entered. “’ntrammo” is an apocopated form of “entrammo,” first-person plural past historic, and “sanz’ alcuna guerra” means without any resistance or conflict.
segments.inferno-9-g106: “E io” begins a coordinated clause and introduces Dante as the subject of the following main action. The phrase is suspended by the relative clause that follows before the verb “invio” appears.
segments.inferno-9-g107: “ch’avea” is a relative clause modifying “io”: “who had.” The object is arranged poetically: Dante “had desire of looking at” the “condizion,” and “che tal fortezza serra” is another relative clause, “which such a fortress encloses.”
segments.inferno-9-g108: “com’ io fui dentro” means “as soon as I was inside”; “come” has a temporal force here, not merely comparative. “Fui” is the past historic of “essere,” marking the completed moment of entry.
segments.inferno-9-g109: “l’occhio intorno invio” has elevated poetic diction and word order: literally, “I send the eye around.” “Occhio” stands metonymically for Dante’s gaze, and “invio” is present tense used vividly after the past-tense entry.
segments.inferno-9-g110: “veggio” is an archaic form of “vedo,” “I see.” “Ad ogne man” literally means “at every hand,” an idiom meaning “on every side,” followed by the object “grande campagna.”
segments.inferno-9-g111: “piena” agrees with “campagna” and completes the description of what Dante sees. The two “di” phrases give what fills the plain: grief and cruel torment.
segments.inferno-9-g112: “Sì come” introduces the first comparison, “just as.” “Ad Arli” means “at Arles,” with “ad” before a vowel and “Arli” as Dante’s form of the place-name Arles.
segments.inferno-9-g113: “ove” introduces a relative place clause, “where.” “Rodano” is the Rhône River, and “stagna” means that its waters stagnate or lie still near the marshy mouth.
segments.inferno-9-g114: A second comparison is added in parallel with the first: “sì com’ a Pola,” “just as at Pola.” The repetition of “sì come” builds the simile before the main clause arrives.
segments.inferno-9-g115: “presso del Carnaro” locates Pola near the Quarnaro/Kvarner Gulf. The relative clause “ch’Italia chiude e suoi termini bagna” describes that gulf as bounding Italy and washing its borders; “suoi” refers to Italy’s.
segments.inferno-9-g116: This is the main clause of the two comparisons: “the sepulchers make the whole place varo.” “Varo” here is an archaic adjective meaning uneven, varied, or broken up, though the supplied senses do not include that meaning.
segments.inferno-9-g117: “così” answers the earlier “sì come”: as tombs make those places uneven, so the tombs did here. “facevan” is an apocopated imperfect form, “they were making/did make,” and “d’ogne parte” means “on every side.”
segments.inferno-9-g118: “salvo che” means “except that,” introducing the difference between Hell and the earthly cemeteries. “v’era” is “there was,” with “vi” contracted before “era,” and “più amaro” means more painful or bitter.
segments.inferno-9-g119: “ché” gives the reason for the greater bitterness: “for/because.” “tra li avelli” means “among the tombs,” and “fiamme erano sparte” is a passive construction, “flames were scattered,” with “sparte” from “spargere.”
segments.inferno-9-g120: “per le quali” refers back to the flames and means “by means of which.” “eran sì del tutto accesi” uses “sì” as an intensifier leading to the following result clause: they were so completely set ablaze.
segments.inferno-9-g121: “che” introduces the result of the preceding “sì”: they were so hot that no craft asks for iron to be hotter. “ferro” is a noun here, “iron,” and “verun’ arte” means “any art/craft,” especially metalworking.
segments.inferno-9-g122: “Tutti li lor coperchi” means “all their lids,” with “li” as the old plural article and “lor” as the possessive “their.” “eran sospesi” is an imperfect passive or stative expression: the lids were hanging open or held up.
segments.inferno-9-g123: “fuor n’uscivan” means “out from them came forth,” where “n’” is contracted “ne,” referring back to the tombs, not a negative. “sì duri lamenti” means “such harsh laments,” with “sì” preparing the result clause that follows.
segments.inferno-9-g124: “che” introduces the result of “sì”: the laments were so harsh that they seemed to be those of sufferers. “ben parean” means “clearly/seemingly indeed appeared,” and “di miseri e d’offesi” means “of wretched and injured people.”
segments.inferno-9-g125: “E io” introduces Dante’s speech after the description. The verb of speaking is omitted, a common narrative ellipsis: “And I [said].”
segments.inferno-9-g126: “Maestro” is a vocative, addressed to Virgil. It carries the sense of “Master” or authoritative guide rather than simply a schoolteacher.
segments.inferno-9-g127: “quai son” is an archaic/poetic form of “quali sono,” meaning “who/what are.” “quelle genti che” sets up the noun “people” followed by a relative clause describing them.
segments.inferno-9-g128: “seppellite” is a feminine plural past participle agreeing with “genti,” used in a reduced relative phrase: “who are buried.” “dentro da quell’ arche” means “inside those tombs,” with “arche” used for sepulchral chests or tombs.
segments.inferno-9-g129: “si fan sentir” is a reflexive causative construction: literally, “they make themselves be heard.” “coi sospiri dolenti” gives the means by which they are heard: through sorrowful sighs.

sentences.inferno-9-s6: Such a one offered herself to us.
sentences.inferno-9-s7: Oh, how long it seems to me before someone else arrives here!
sentences.inferno-9-s1: That color which cowardice had painted outwardly on me, when I saw my guide turn back, more quickly repressed within him his own new color.
sentences.inferno-9-s2: He stopped, intent, like a man who listens; for his sight could not lead him far through the dark air and through the thick fog.
sentences.inferno-9-s3: “Nevertheless, we shall have to overcome the struggle,” he began, “unless—”
sentences.inferno-9-s29: I clearly realized that he had been sent from heaven, and I turned to my master; and he made a sign that I should remain quiet and bow to him.
sentences.inferno-9-s30: Ah, how full of disdain he seemed to me!
sentences.inferno-9-s31: He came to the gate and opened it with a little rod, for there was no resistance there.
sentences.inferno-9-s32: “O you cast out of heaven, despised people,” he began upon the horrible threshold, “whence is this arrogance lodged in you?”
sentences.inferno-9-s9: I saw clearly how he covered over the beginning with the other words that then came, which were words different from the first; but nevertheless his speech gave us fear, because I drew the truncated word perhaps to a worse meaning than it held.
sentences.inferno-9-s10: “Into this depth of the wretched basin does anyone ever descend from the first circle, whose only punishment is hope cut off?”
sentences.inferno-9-s12: I asked this question; and he replied to me, “Rarely does it happen that any one of us makes the journey by which I go.
sentences.inferno-9-s21: “Let Medusa come: then we shall make him enamel-hard,” they all were saying, looking downward; “we did wrong not to avenge the assault upon Theseus.”
sentences.inferno-9-s22: “Turn around and keep your face covered; for if the Gorgon shows herself and you should see her, there would be no returning upward ever.”
sentences.inferno-9-s23: Thus spoke the master; and he himself turned me around, and he did not trust to my hands alone, but with his own also covered my eyes.
sentences.inferno-9-s24: O you who have sound minds, look closely at the teaching that hides beneath the veil of the strange verses.
sentences.inferno-9-s13: It is true that on another occasion I was down here, conjured by that cruel Erichtho, who used to call the shades back to their bodies.
sentences.inferno-9-s14: Only a short time had my flesh been stripped of me when she made me enter within that wall, to draw out from there a spirit from the circle of Judas.
sentences.inferno-9-s15: That is the lowest place and the darkest, and the farthest from the heaven that turns all; I know the path well; therefore make yourself confident.
sentences.inferno-9-s16: This marsh, which breathes out the great stench, encircles the sorrowful city, where by now we cannot enter without anger.
sentences.inferno-9-s42: And he said to me: “Here are the heresiarchs with their followers, of every sect, and the tombs are much fuller than you think.”
sentences.inferno-9-s43: Here like is buried with like, and the tombs are hotter and less hot.”
sentences.inferno-9-s44: And after he had turned to the right-hand side, we passed between the torments and the high ramparts.
sentences.inferno-9-s17: And he said something else, but I do not have it in mind, because my gaze had drawn me completely toward the high tower with its glowing summit, where in an instant three infernal Furies, stained with blood, stood swiftly upright; they had female limbs and bearing, and were girded with very green hydras; they had little serpents and horned vipers for hair, with which their fierce temples were entwined.
sentences.inferno-9-s18: And he, who well recognized the handmaids of the queen of eternal lamentation, said to me, “Look at the fierce Erinyes.”
sentences.inferno-9-s19: “This is Megaera on the left side; the one who weeps on the right is Alecto; Tisiphone is in the middle”; and at that he fell silent.
sentences.inferno-9-s20: With their nails each one tore her breast; they beat themselves with their palms and cried out so loudly that I pressed close to the poet out of fear.
sentences.inferno-9-s25: And already, up over the turbid waves, there came a crash of sound, full of terror, at which both banks trembled, made just like that of a wind made violent by opposing heats, which strikes the forest and, without any restraint, splinters the branches, beats them down, and carries them away; dusty, it goes proudly ahead, and makes the wild beasts and the shepherds flee.
sentences.inferno-9-s26: He freed my eyes and said: “Now direct the strength of your sight up over that ancient foam, there where that smoke is most bitter.”
sentences.inferno-9-s27: As frogs before their enemy the snake all scatter through the water, until each one huddles on the land, so I saw more than a thousand ruined souls flee before one who, at the ford, was crossing Styx with dry soles.
sentences.inferno-9-s28: From his face he was clearing away that thick air, often moving his left hand in front of him; and he seemed weary only from that discomfort.
sentences.inferno-9-s33: Why do you resist that Will, whose purpose can never be cut short, and which has many times increased your pain?
sentences.inferno-9-s34: What good is it to butt against the fates?
sentences.inferno-9-s35: Your Cerberus, if you remember well, still bears his chin and throat flayed from it.
sentences.inferno-9-s36: Then he turned back along the filthy road, and did not say a word to us, but wore the look of a man whom some other care grips and bites than care for the person before him; and we set our feet toward the city, confident after the holy words.
sentences.inferno-9-s37: We entered into it without any struggle; and I, who had a longing to look upon the condition that such a fortress encloses, as soon as I was inside, sent my gaze around: and I saw on every side a great plain, full of grief and harsh torment.
sentences.inferno-9-s38: Just as at Arles, where the Rhône stagnates, and just as at Pola, near the Quarnaro that bounds Italy and bathes its borders, the tombs make the whole place uneven, so they did there on every side, except that the manner there was more bitter; for among the sepulchers flames were scattered, by which they were so wholly set ablaze that no craft demands iron any hotter.
sentences.inferno-9-s39: All their lids were suspended, and from them came forth such harsh laments that they clearly seemed to belong to wretched and injured people.
sentences.inferno-9-s40: And I said: “Master, who are those people who, buried inside those tombs, make themselves heard with their sorrowful sighs?”
